prompt:
Triage duty keeps needing the same five 'which files' questions answered on our zsh 5.9 boxes, and everyone answers them with a different mangled find(1) one-liner. I want one small zsh script that answers them with the shell's own glob machinery — that's the whole point, the boxes have zsh and its glob qualifiers do all of this natively: (.) for plain files, (/) for directories, (L+N) for size, (om) for mtime ordering, (m+N) for age, a [1,K] subscript right on the glob for 'top K'. No find, no stat parsing, no ls.

File: `pick.zsh` in the repo root, run as `zsh pick.zsh <mode> <dir> [N]`. Every mode searches DIR recursively and prints matches one per line, as paths relative to DIR (never './'-prefixed, never absolute). Hidden files and hidden directories stay invisible, same as a normal glob.

Modes:
- `files <dir>` — regular files only, name order.
- `dirs <dir>` — directories only, name order.
- `over <dir> <bytes>` — regular files whose size is STRICTLY greater than <bytes> bytes, name order. A 600-byte file is not 'over 600'.
- `newest <dir> <k>` — the k most recently modified regular files, newest first. Fewer than k exist: print them all. k is 0: that's an empty result.
- `stale <dir> <days>` — regular files last modified more than <days> days ago, name order.

Name order means plain byte order (we run under LC_ALL=C); make the ordering explicit with the (on) qualifier rather than trusting defaults. For newest, the order is (om)'s — newest first — not name order.

Empty result (any mode): print NOTHING on either stream and exit 1. Careful here — zsh aborts the whole script with a 'no matches found' error when a glob comes up empty, so the no-match case has to be handled deliberately (the N qualifier), not left to luck. The harness checks that stderr stays clean.

Errors, all with empty stdout:
- run with no arguments at all: just the usage line on stderr, exit 64.
- unknown mode: `pick.zsh: unknown mode: <mode>` then the usage line, exit 64.
- right mode, wrong argument count: `pick.zsh: wrong number of arguments` then the usage line, exit 64.
- N that isn't a non-negative integer (12x, -1, ...): `pick.zsh: not a number: <N>` then the usage line, exit 64.
- DIR that isn't a directory: `pick.zsh: not a directory: <dir>` on stderr (no usage line), exit 66.

The usage line, byte-exact: `usage: pick.zsh files|dirs|over|newest|stale <dir> [N]`

Acceptance is `zsh test_globpick.zsh` from the workspace root; it builds its own fixture tree (exact byte sizes, pinned past mtimes) and must pass unmodified. Don't touch the test file.
